title: The Root Verses on the Six Bardos
section: Bardo of This Life
author: Karma Lingpa
text: Kyema! Now when the bardo of this life is dawning upon me, / I will abandon laziness for which life has no time, / Enter, undistracted, the path of study, reflection and meditation, / Making perceptions and mind the path, and realize the three kāyas; / Now that for once I have attained a human body, / This is not the time to remain in the ways of distraction.
summary: The verse instructs the practitioner to use the bardo of this life by abandoning distraction and engaging earnestly in study, reflection, and meditation in order to realize the three kāyas.
notes: Directly introduces the six-bardo framework central to this project. The phrase 'making perceptions and mind the path' links phenomenal appearances to consciousness, key for co-occurrence analysis. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016. Source: Karma Lingpa's terma, part of the Liberation Upon Hearing in the Bardo (Tibetan Book of the Dead).

title: The Root Verses on the Six Bardos
section: Bardo of Dreams
author: Karma Lingpa
text: Kyema! Now when the bardo of dreams is dawning upon me, / I will abandon the heedless, corpse-like sleep of ignorance, / And settle the mind in its natural state without distraction; / Recognizing dreams, I will train in transformation and clear light, / I must not simply slumber like an animal, / But combine sleep with realization—this is crucial.
summary: The dream bardo verse instructs the practitioner to recognize dreams, train in the transformation of dream content, and cultivate clear light rather than remaining in heedless sleep.
notes: Dream recognition as parallel to bardo recognition is important for co-occurrence analysis around 'recognition,' 'clear light,' and 'natural state.' Links to the dying process through the analogy of unconscious sleep. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: The Root Verses on the Six Bardos
section: Bardo of Samādhi Meditation
author: Karma Lingpa
text: Kyema! Now when the bardo of samādhi is dawning upon me, / I will abandon all forms of distraction and delusion, / And rest in the infinite state that is free of distraction and grasping; / Gaining stability in the two stages: generation and perfection, / At this time of single-pointed meditation, having given up activity, / I must not fall under the sway of afflictions and delusion.
summary: The samādhi bardo verse calls for resting in non-grasping awareness and gaining stability in both generation and completion stages of meditation to avoid delusion.
notes: Co-occurrence potential: 'samādhi,' 'delusion,' 'distraction,' 'grasping,' and 'awareness.' Establishes that meditative experience in life directly conditions bardo recognition. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: The Root Verses on the Six Bardos
section: Bardo of Dying
author: Karma Lingpa
text: Kyema! Now when the bardo of dying is dawning upon me, / I will abandon all grasping, yearning, and attachment, / Enter, undistracted, a state in which the instructions are clear, / And transfer my own awareness into the sphere of unborn space; / As I am about to leave this compound body of flesh and blood, / I will know it to be a transitory illusion.
summary: The dying bardo verse instructs the practitioner to release all attachment at death, clearly recall the instructions, and transfer awareness into unborn space, understanding the body as an impermanent illusion.
notes: Central section for the research question: covers dying, attachment, transference of awareness (phowa), and the body as illusion. Key vocabulary: 'awareness,' 'unborn space,' 'grasping,' 'instructions.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: The Root Verses on the Six Bardos
section: Bardo of Dharmatā
author: Karma Lingpa
text: Kyema! Now when the bardo of dharmatā is dawning upon me, / I will abandon all fear and terror, / Recognizing whatever appears as the natural display of awareness, / I will know it to be the way this bardo unfolds; / Now that I have reached this momentous, crucial point, / I will not fear these natural manifestations, the peaceful and wrathful deities.
summary: The dharmatā bardo verse instructs the practitioner to recognize all arising appearances—including the peaceful and wrathful deities—as the natural display of awareness and to overcome fear through recognition.
notes: Directly addresses dharmatā appearances, recognition, and the peaceful/wrathful deities. Key co-occurrence vocabulary: 'dharmatā,' 'awareness,' 'appearances,' 'recognition,' 'fear,' 'natural display.' Critical for the research question about how appearances in the bardo lead to liberation or rebirth. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: The Root Verses on the Six Bardos
section: Bardo of Becoming
author: Karma Lingpa
text: Kyema! Now when the bardo of becoming is dawning upon me, / I will concentrate my mind with single-pointed determination, / Strive to prolong the results of good karma, / Close the entrance to rebirth, and try to keep from being reborn. / This is the time when perseverance and pure perception are required; / Abandon jealousy, and meditate on the master and consort.
summary: The becoming bardo verse calls for concentrated mind, strong positive karma, and the closing of the womb door to prevent unfavorable rebirth, emphasizing devotion and pure perception.
notes: Directly addresses the bardo of becoming and rebirth prevention. Key vocabulary: 'rebirth,' 'karma,' 'womb door,' 'pure perception,' 'becoming.' Central to research on how the transition from death to rebirth is described. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: Crucial Advice: A Complete Set of Instructions for the Bardos
section: Bardo of This Life and Dzogchen View
author: Longchen Rabjam
text: Although you have gained this life of freedom and advantage, it will not last, / So keep in mind these instructions for the moment of death. / Now, during this intermediate period of the bardo of this life, / Decide, with complete certainty, that the wisdom of your own awareness is dharmakāya, / And sustaining the ongoing experience of its self-radiance, the meditation which is naturally clear, / Everything will only enhance naturally arising wisdom!
summary: Longchen Rabjam opens by urging urgency in death preparation and instructs the practitioner to recognize their own awareness as dharmakāya during this life and sustain natural meditation.
notes: Establishes the Dzogchen view that awareness equals dharmakāya—central to understanding liberation at death. Key co-occurrence: 'awareness,' 'dharmakāya,' 'self-radiance,' 'naturally arising wisdom.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010. Dedicated to memory of Ian Maxwell.

title: Crucial Advice: A Complete Set of Instructions for the Bardos
section: Bardo of Dying and Elemental Dissolution
author: Longchen Rabjam
text: During the bardo of dying, when the four elements dissolve, / There will be the illusory experiences of rising and falling, shaking, and haziness. / And the dissolution of earth, water, fire, wind and space. / The sense faculties too will cease to function. At that time, remind yourself: / 'Now I am dying, but there is no need to fear.' / Examine: 'What is death? Who is dying? Where does dying take place?'
summary: During the bardo of dying Longchen Rabjam describes elemental dissolution and instructs the practitioner to examine the nature of death directly rather than fear it.
notes: Rich vocabulary for the dissolution sequence: 'elements dissolve,' 'sense faculties cease,' 'dying,' 'fear.' The philosophical question 'Who is dying?' establishes non-self inquiry central to liberation. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: Crucial Advice: A Complete Set of Instructions for the Bardos
section: Recognition of Clear Light and Liberation at Death
author: Longchen Rabjam
text: Death is merely the return of borrowed elements. / In the face of rigpa itself, there is no birth or death. / Separated from the ordinary mind, the great primordial purity of dharmakāya dawns. / Through having recognized this here and now in training, / You will be freed directly, in a single instant. / And gain the dharmakāya of twofold purity.
summary: Longchen Rabjam teaches that the moment of death offers an opportunity to recognize dharmakāya clear light and attain liberation in a single instant through prior meditation training.
notes: Pivotal passage for the research question: 'rigpa,' 'dharmakāya,' 'primordial purity,' 'liberation,' 'clear light' are all concentrated here. Establishes the relationship between dying and liberation through recognition. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: Crucial Advice: A Complete Set of Instructions for the Bardos
section: Bardo of Dharmatā and Appearances as Rigpa
author: Longchen Rabjam
text: Thereafter, clear light appearances—manifestations of the ground—will arise. / Sounds, lights and colours, peaceful and wrathful ones filling the sky, / By recognizing all these appearances as rigpa's self-radiance, / You will be freed in the original state, and attain awakening. / It is crucial, therefore, to recognize everything as intrinsic radiance. / Through recognizing the essence, you will gain enlightenment.
summary: After the moment of death, ground-appearances of sound, light, and color arise in the bardo of dharmatā; recognizing them as rigpa's own radiance leads to liberation.
notes: Key for co-occurrence: 'appearances,' 'rigpa,' 'self-radiance,' 'sounds,' 'lights,' 'peaceful and wrathful,' 'recognition,' 'liberation.' Directly describes how dharmatā appearances function in the transition between dying and rebirth. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: Crucial Advice: A Complete Set of Instructions for the Bardos
section: Bardo of Becoming and Aspiration
author: Longchen Rabjam
text: This is how it all arises, but should you fail to recognize it, / The dream-like bardo of becoming will dawn. / At that time, by recalling a pure land, / And taking refuge in the lama and the yidam deity, / Some will find freedom in a pure buddha paradise, / And some will gain the seven qualities of birth in a higher realm, / And be assured of gaining liberation in the next life.
summary: If recognition fails in the dharmatā bardo, the becoming bardo arises; devotion to lama and yidam and aspiration for a pure land can still lead to liberation in a future life.
notes: Covers failure to recognize, dream-like becoming bardo, pure land aspiration, and rebirth in higher realms. Key vocabulary: 'bardo of becoming,' 'pure land,' 'liberation,' 'rebirth.' Completes the three-bardo sequence. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: Liberation Upon Hearing: An Explanation of the Phenomena of the Intermediate State
section: General Teaching: Essence and Meaning of Bardo
author: Minling Terchen Gyurme Dorje
text: Kye! Son or daughter of noble family, you, the one named [insert name], who have died, do not be distracted! Listen and keep this in mind. It is the general nature of all conditioned phenomena to be impermanent and subject to change. And it is taught that the nature of beings' lives in particular is that they lack even so much as a moment's permanence. For you, the time has now come: your life is spent. You are in the intermediate state. Hold to these key points of profound instruction, and until this ritual introduction is complete maintain a natural state of awareness.
summary: The text opens by addressing the deceased by name, emphasizing impermanence and urging them not to be distracted but to hold a natural state of awareness in the intermediate state.
notes: Written to be recited aloud at death—ideal for studying the ritual guidance register and the vocabulary of address to the deceased. Key words: 'natural state,' 'awareness,' 'intermediate state,' 'impermanent,' 'distracted.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: Liberation Upon Hearing: An Explanation of the Phenomena of the Intermediate State
section: Divisions of Bardo and Specific Practices
author: Minling Terchen Gyurme Dorje
text: The term bardo refers to an interim between what comes before and what follows. Thus, there are four: 1) the bardo of birth and death or natural bardo; 2) the bardo of dying; 3) the dharmatā bardo; and 4) the bardo of becoming. The first begins with birth from the mother's womb and continues until succumbing to a fatal condition or injury. The second begins with the approach of death and continues until breathing ceases. The third begins with the cessation of inner respiration and continues until the dissolution of the visions of spontaneous presence. The fourth begins with the dissolution of the visions of spontaneous presence and continues until the occurrence of a subsequent birth.
summary: The text provides clear definitions of all four bardos and specifies the exact boundaries of each, from birth through dying, dharmatā, and becoming, up to the next rebirth.
notes: Provides the clearest definitional language for all four bardos in the corpus—essential for understanding how each term is used across texts. Key vocabulary: 'bardo,' 'dying,' 'dharmatā,' 'spontaneous presence,' 'subsequent birth.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: Liberation Upon Hearing: An Explanation of the Phenomena of the Intermediate State
section: Bardo of Dying and Elemental Dissolution
author: Minling Terchen Gyurme Dorje
text: At the time of transferring from life at death, the body's water, fire and wind elements are disturbed and it can no longer support heat or consciousness. This marks the severing of one's vital force. The aggregates, elements and sense-sources, together with their objects, all gradually fade. When earth dissolves into water the body's strength deteriorates. As water dissolves into fire, the mouth and nostrils dry up. As fire dissolves into wind, the body loses heat beginning from the extremities. As wind dissolves into consciousness, the flow of coarse breathing is cut. As consciousness dissolves into space, all the coming and goings of energy-mind cease.
summary: The bardo of dying is described through the sequential dissolution of the four elements and then consciousness into space, with each stage marked by specific physiological and experiential signs.
notes: The most detailed dissolution sequence in the corpus. Key vocabulary: 'dissolves,' 'elements,' 'consciousness,' 'space,' 'aggregates,' 'sense-sources,' 'vital force.' Critical for the research question on how dying is described. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: Liberation Upon Hearing: An Explanation of the Phenomena of the Intermediate State
section: Bardo of Dharmatā and Sambhogakāya Visions
author: Minling Terchen Gyurme Dorje
text: This is the time when luminosity dissolves into union. Out of that comes the increase of experience and out of that, appearance. Then, in an instant, from this threefold experience and its accompanying wind-energy, through the proximate cause of the appearance of clear light there arises an extremely subtle form, clear and unobstructed, like a reflection in a mirror or rainbow light in the sky. This is the dawning of saṃbhogakāya. On the first day, the maṇḍala of Vairocana arises out of blue light, which is like a cloudless autumn sky. Similarly, on the following four days, the other sets of maṇḍala gathering arise on a boundless scale.
summary: As the dharmatā bardo begins, the sambhogakāya arises from clear light as an extremely subtle form; over five days, the mandalas of the five buddha families appear in sequences of colored light.
notes: Critical for research: describes how appearances in dharmatā arise from consciousness/clear light and how the five families' mandala unfolds. Key vocabulary: 'luminosity,' 'union,' 'sambhogakāya,' 'clear light,' 'appearances,' 'five families.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: Liberation Upon Hearing: An Explanation of the Phenomena of the Intermediate State
section: Bardo of Becoming and Guidance for Rebirth
author: Minling Terchen Gyurme Dorje
text: For beginners the earlier visions of luminous dharmatā fade away unrecognised. Then, in the next moment, habitual tendencies for the following stage are triggered by various activating conditions. Wind-energy, mind and the four subtle elements serve as causes and conditions through which the subtle and unobstructed bardo form is created. The duration of this bardo is seven days, where one day is equivalent to a human day. Then, if one has still not found the support of another body, one dies again and lives for another seven days. This process of repeated birth and death every seven days continues for a maximum of forty-nine days in total.
summary: When dharmatā visions go unrecognized, the becoming bardo arises; a subtle mental body forms through habitual tendencies and karma, and the being experiences repeated birth-and-death cycles within forty-nine days.
notes: Describes the becoming bardo's mechanics: habitual tendencies, mental body, forty-nine days, repeated dying. Key vocabulary: 'bardo of becoming,' 'habitual tendencies,' 'mental body,' 'rebirth,' 'karma,' 'consciousness.' Central to research on becoming/rebirth. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: A Brief Introduction to the Bardos
section: Twenty Phases of Coarse Dissolution
author: Patrul Rinpoche
text: Generally, whenever physically embodied beings die, they first experience the twenty phases of coarse dissolution, which are as follows: As the aggregate of form dissolves, the limbs twitch, and the body loses its strength and power. As the mirror-like wisdom dissolves, the mind grows unclear and hazy. As the earth element dissolves, the body grows dry. As the eye faculty dissolves, sight becomes unclear and the eye takes on a rounder shape. As the water element dissolves, the lips, sweat, urine, semen and ovum all dry up. As the fire element dissolves, proper digestion of food is no longer possible.
summary: Patrul Rinpoche enumerates twenty coarse stages of dissolution at death, covering the dissolution of aggregates, elements, sense faculties, and their objects in a systematic sequence.
notes: The most exhaustive dissolution checklist in the corpus—vital for word frequency of dissolution-related verbs and bodily processes. Key vocabulary: 'dissolves,' 'aggregate,' 'element,' 'faculty,' 'wisdom.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: A Brief Introduction to the Bardos
section: Subtle Inner Dissolution of the Four Elements
author: Patrul Rinpoche
text: Following the twenty coarse forms of dissolution, which unfold in this way, comes the process of subtle inner dissolution: As earth dissolves into water, the person is unable to move the body and can no longer maintain its strength. It feels as if the body is sinking into the ground. As an inner sign, there is a shimmering expanse of blue, and the impression of softly drizzling rain and flowing water. As water dissolves into fire, the mouth and nostrils dry up, and the tongue becomes lodged against the palate. As an inner sign, there is an appearance of a smoky haze swirling on a plain.
summary: Following the coarse dissolution, Patrul Rinpoche describes the subtle inner elemental dissolution with accompanying outer signs (body symptoms) and inner signs (visionary experiences like smoke, mirages, sparks, and a burning lamp).
notes: Describes both outer and inner signs of elemental dissolution—important for co-occurrence of 'signs,' 'elements,' 'inner,' 'consciousness,' 'appearances.' The sign language parallels the root verses. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: A Brief Introduction to the Bardos
section: Four Visions: Appearance Increase Attainment and Ground Luminosity
author: Patrul Rinpoche
text: Then, as space dissolves into luminosity, four visions gradually unfold: The white essence obtained from the father descends from the crown, and when it reaches the heart there is what is called 'appearance'. As an outer sign, this is accompanied by an experience of whiteness, as when moonlight fills a completely clear sky. As an inner sign, there is a clear experience of the self-clarity of consciousness devoid of any coarse thought states. When the subtle red element of the mother ascends from the base of the central channel, the wisdom of appearance fades into 'increase'. The wisdom of increase dissolves into 'attainment'. The ground luminosity of the moment of death arises. As an outer sign, there is an experience of emptiness and clarity without centre or periphery, like a cloudless sky.
summary: The four visions of appearance, increase, attainment, and ground luminosity unfold as the white and red elements meet at the heart; ground luminosity is the opportunity for liberation through recognition.
notes: Central to the research: describes the three experiences plus luminosity, white/red essences, emptiness, and the recognition opportunity. Key vocabulary: 'luminosity,' 'appearance,' 'increase,' 'attainment,' 'consciousness,' 'liberation,' 'recognition.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: A Brief Introduction to the Bardos
section: Bardo of Becoming and Its Features
author: Patrul Rinpoche
text: Next, out of the luminosity into which the three visions dissolved, appearances unfold gradually once again, and the body of the bardo of becoming is formed. For the first half of this bardo, however long that might be, you have the form of your preceding life, and for the second half you take on the appearance of your eventual rebirth. All sensory faculties are intact, and you can travel anywhere but the mother's womb, miraculously and without obstruction. You are invisible to all except those of the same class (i.e. other bardo beings) and those who have obtained divine vision.
summary: After the luminosity phase, a subtle becoming-bardo body forms from the dissolving three visions, retaining the previous life's form at first and then shifting to the next rebirth form, with miraculous powers but no shadow.
notes: Describes the becoming bardo body's properties—key for co-occurrence of 'bardo,' 'becoming,' 'rebirth,' 'consciousness,' 'appearances.' Also lists the four fear-inducing sounds as signs of the three lower-realm abysses. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: A Brief Introduction to the Bardos
section: Practical Guidance at the Moment of Death
author: Patrul Rinpoche
text: At first, when you are certain that you are going to die, you must cut all ties and attachment to this life. Confess from the depths of your heart any downfalls and breakages of samaya, harmful actions and so on. Devote not even so much as a single moment to feeling guilty about your own negative actions, fearing death, or being attached to this life. Instead, feel happiness and joy, and say to yourself: 'Now I shall recognize the clear light at death. Or, if that is not possible, since I shall certainly use the bardo as an opportunity to travel to a pure realm such as Akaniṣṭha, Zangdokpalri or Sukhāvatī, I shall be joyful.' Maintain, without ever letting it slip away, the strong intention and thought 'I shall travel to the pure realms!'
summary: Patrul Rinpoche gives practical guidance for the moment of dying: release attachment, confess, cultivate joy, and strongly aspire to recognize clear light or travel to a pure realm.
notes: Practical instruction for dying practitioners—key vocabulary: 'clear light,' 'bardo,' 'pure realm,' 'liberation,' 'attachment,' 'recognition,' 'aspiration.' Useful for studying the vocabulary of ritual guidance at the moment of death. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2010.

title: The Flight of the Garuda: An Aspiration for the Four Bardos
section: Opening Invocation and Renunciation Aspiration
author: Second Shechen Rabjam
text: Guide Amitābha, Lord Avalokiteśvara, Vajrasattva, / The Five Families, the Hundred Families, Lord Padmasambhava, / The Three Roots, peaceful and wrathful deities, and Three Supreme Jewels— / From my heart I pray to you: please grant your blessings! / With a mind that is swift to recall impermanence and death, / May I see saṃsāra's pleasures and wealth as dances of lightning, / Or as insubstantial as a rainbow in the sky. / Bless me that such urgency may arise in my mind.
summary: The prayer opens with invocations to Amitābha, Avalokiteśvara, the five buddha families, and Padmasambhava, then aspires for vivid recognition of impermanence and the insubstantiality of samsaric existence.
notes: Establishes the full array of refuge objects for bardo aspiration. Co-occurrence vocabulary: 'Amitābha,' 'peaceful and wrathful deities,' 'impermanence,' 'death,' 'saṃsāra,' 'liberation.' Composed 1758. Translator: Sean Price, 2026.

title: The Flight of the Garuda: An Aspiration for the Four Bardos
section: Coarse Elemental Dissolution at Death
author: Second Shechen Rabjam
text: When the aggregates, four wisdoms, four elements, / Four faculties, and four objects—these twenty coarse factors— / Dissolve, and the sensory consciousnesses withdraw inward, / May my mind remain unwavering and confident. / As earth dissolves into water, the body's vitality diminishes, / Leaving one unable to move and feeling as if falling into a pit. / And smoke-like mist or haziness appears. / May I recognise the earth-wind dissolving into the central channel.
summary: At the onset of dying, twenty coarse factors dissolve in sequence; the prayer aspires that the practitioner remain unwavering and recognize each dissolution of the four elements as they dissolve into the central channel.
notes: Aspiration prayer framing of the dissolution process—useful for comparing the vocabulary of dissolution across instruction genres. Key terms: 'dissolves,' 'elements,' 'central channel,' 'consciousness,' 'recognition.' Translator: Sean Price, 2026.

title: The Flight of the Garuda: An Aspiration for the Four Bardos
section: Subtle Dissolution: Three Experiences and Ground Luminosity
author: Second Shechen Rabjam
text: From the haṃ at the crown, the white element dissolves / Into the drop at the centre of the heart. The outer sign is whiteness, / And the inner sign is clearer than before, arising without fixation. / The red short a from the navel ascends / And dissolves into the drop at the heart. The outer sign is redness, / While the inner sign is an extremely subtle clarity, the inseparability of wind and mind. / That very nature, primordially pure, is empty in essence; / Its natural expression is luminous bliss without reference point. / May the dharmakāya luminosity of the moment of death become manifest.
summary: The prayer describes the white and red essences descending and ascending to the heart, the three wisdoms of appearance/increase/attainment, and the arising of dharmakāya luminosity at death.
notes: Rich vocabulary for the subtle dissolution: 'white element,' 'red element,' 'heart,' 'luminosity,' 'dharmakāya,' 'emptiness,' 'primordial purity.' Aspiration form provides prayer-genre parallel to instruction texts. Translator: Sean Price, 2026.

title: The Flight of the Garuda: An Aspiration for the Four Bardos
section: Bardo of Dharmatā: Visions and Recognition
author: Second Shechen Rabjam
text: Then, in the bardo of dharmatā, / When space dissolves into luminosity, / There are the four manifestations: the appearances of the primordial ground, / The union of spheric clusters, the union of four wisdoms, and great spontaneous presence. / First, when the appearances of the primordial ground arise, / Five-coloured rainbow light rays from the wisdom spheres / Of the five lights in the sky before me connect with my eyes. / May I recognising this as the cord of continual compassion, / The direct and secret path of Vajrasattva, / And by focusing awareness undistractedly and single-pointedly upon it, / May mother and child dharmatās merge instantaneously.
summary: The dharmatā bardo brings forth the four manifestations—ground appearances, spheric clusters, four wisdoms, and spontaneous presence—as five-colored light; the prayer aspires for recognition as one's own nature.
notes: Most detailed description of dharmatā visions in prayer form: 'five-coloured light,' 'dharmatā,' 'recognition,' 'mother and child,' 'awareness,' 'liberation.' Important for both co-occurrence analysis and genre comparison. Translator: Sean Price, 2026.

title: The Flight of the Garuda: An Aspiration for the Four Bardos
section: Bardo of Becoming and Liberation Aspiration
author: Second Shechen Rabjam
text: If not liberated into the sambhogakāya during the dharmatā bardo, / Even though the bardo of becoming appears like a dream, / Through the power of familiarity with the supreme guru, / Profound instructions, and direct experience of luminous dharmatā, / May I recall the pure realms and at that very moment, / Be born in the Abhirati realm, and behold the face of Akṣobhya; / In Śrīmat, receive empowerment from Ratnasambhava; / In Padmakūṭa, receive prophecy from Amitābha.
summary: If the dharmatā bardo passes unrecognized, the prayer aspires that in the dream-like becoming bardo the practitioner will recall a pure realm—specifically one of the five buddha-family pure lands—and be reborn there for liberation.
notes: Covers the failure cascade from dharmatā to becoming bardo and the aspiration for pure land rebirth. Key vocabulary: 'bardo of becoming,' 'sambhogakāya,' 'pure realms,' 'liberation,' 'rebirth,' 'dharmatā.' Translator: Sean Price, 2026.

title: How Liberation and Delusion Develop Out of the Clear Light of the Ground
section: The Youthful Vase Body and Ground Clear Light
author: Yukhok Chatralwa Chöying Rangdrol
text: In the tradition of the Clear Light Great Perfection, it is said that the subtle wisdom we call the 'youthful vase body'—an inner clarity that is immanent and unobscured—is present in the mindstreams of all living beings. This youthful vase body is the wisdom of uncompounded clear light, which is 'youthful' in the sense that it is beyond birth and death, ageing and decay. It is a 'vase body' in that the seal of spontaneous presence remains unbroken. It is an inner clarity, which does not obstruct the kāyas and wisdoms. Obscuration by the three kinds of habitual tendency is likened to a vase. And subtle wisdom itself, which has the identity of the three kāyas, is likened to a lamp within this vase.
summary: The text introduces the 'youthful vase body' as the uncompounded clear light wisdom present in all beings, obscured by habitual tendencies like a lamp inside a vase, and identifies it with the three kāyas.
notes: Key Dzogchen technical vocabulary: 'youthful vase body,' 'clear light,' 'habitual tendency,' 'three kāyas,' 'spontaneous presence.' Foundational concept for understanding what is recognized (or not) at the moment of death. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: How Liberation and Delusion Develop Out of the Clear Light of the Ground
section: Liberation in the Bardo of Dying and Dharmatā
author: Yukhok Chatralwa Chöying Rangdrol
text: Following the conclusion of all the stages of dissolution, during the dharmakāya intermediate stage of dying, you remain in the clear light of the ground for five 'meditation days'. When rising from this state, if you recognise the clear light of the ground as your own projection, then, in the first instant, you recognise the natural state of the original, primordial ground of alpha-purity; in the second instant, the recogniser dissolves into absolute space; and in the third instant, by seizing the stronghold within absolute space, you are liberated at death into dharmakāya reality. If you are not liberated in that intermediate state, however, then during the intermediate state of dharmatā, the seal of the youthful vase body breaks, causing the secret gateways of spontaneous presence to open and five-coloured lights to emerge.
summary: After dissolution, the clear light of the ground arises for five meditation days; recognizing it in three instants leads to dharmakāya liberation, while failure triggers the dharmatā bardo with five-colored lights.
notes: Describes the three-instant recognition process and the critical moment of choosing liberation over delusion. Co-occurrence vocabulary: 'clear light,' 'ground,' 'recognition,' 'dissolution,' 'dharmakāya,' 'liberation,' 'dharmatā,' 'five-coloured lights.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: How Liberation and Delusion Develop Out of the Clear Light of the Ground
section: Delusion and the Arising of Saṃsāra
author: Yukhok Chatralwa Chöying Rangdrol
text: If the process unfolds in an impure way: / Dharmatā becomes the basis for the delusion of perceived objects; the five-coloured lights become the basis for the delusion of the environment and inhabitants; and awareness becomes the basis for the delusion of ordinary mind. The five-coloured lights appear as the five elements; spheres appear as ordinary dwelling places; the vajra chains appear as your own body; the eight collections of consciousness appear out of the omniscience of compassionate presence as the ordinary mind, the basis of delusion, by means of the three causes and four conditions; and the various languages of the six classes of beings emerge out of the spontaneous sound of dharmatā. And with this, we experience delusion within the endless cycle of saṃsāra.
summary: If the dharmatā process unfolds impurely, awareness becomes ordinary mind, five-colored lights become the five elements, and the sound of dharmatā becomes language—generating the entire delusive world of the six realms.
notes: Describes exactly how the transition from liberation-possibility to samsaric rebirth occurs through non-recognition. Key vocabulary: 'delusion,' 'dharmatā,' 'awareness,' 'consciousness,' 'elements,' 'saṃsāra,' 'rebirth.' Critical for the research question. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: The Noble Mahāyāna Sūtra: The Wisdom of the Hour of Death
section: Setting: Bodhisattva Ākāśagarbha Questions the Buddha
author: Words of the Buddha
text: Thus I have heard at one time. The Blessed One was dwelling in the palace of the king of gods in Akaniṣṭha and teaching the Dharma to the entire retinue when the bodhisattva mahāsattva Ākāśagarbha prostrated to the Blessed One and asked him the following question, 'O Blessed One, how should a bodhisattva view the mind at the moment of death?'
summary: The sūtra opens at Akaniṣṭha pure land; the bodhisattva Ākāśagarbha asks the Buddha how a bodhisattva should view the mind at the moment of death.
notes: The question 'how should a bodhisattva view the mind at the moment of death?' frames the entire sūtra. Key vocabulary: 'moment of death,' 'mind,' 'bodhisattva,' 'Akaniṣṭha.' Provides canonical Mahāyāna context for the dying process. Translator: Lowell Cook, 2018.

title: The Noble Mahāyāna Sūtra: The Wisdom of the Hour of Death
section: Buddha's Teaching: Five Perceptions at the Moment of Death
author: Words of the Buddha
text: The Blessed One replied, 'Ākāśagarbha, at the time of death, the bodhisattva should cultivate the wisdom of the hour of death. As for wisdom of the hour of death, you should cultivate the perception of insubstantiality since all phenomena are naturally pure. You should cultivate the perception of great compassion since all phenomena are contained within bodhicitta. You should cultivate the perception of referencelessness since all phenomena are naturally luminous. You should cultivate the perception of utter non-attachment since all things are impermanent. You should cultivate the perception of not searching for buddhahood elsewhere since the mind is wisdom when realized.'
summary: The Buddha teaches five perceptions to cultivate at death: insubstantiality, great compassion, referencelessness, non-attachment, and recognizing mind as wisdom—together constituting the 'wisdom of the hour of death.'
notes: Canonical teaching on the five death-moment perceptions. Key vocabulary: 'insubstantiality,' 'luminous,' 'non-attachment,' 'mind is wisdom,' 'moment of death.' Links dying directly to awareness and liberation. Translator: Lowell Cook, 2018.

title: The Noble Mahāyāna Sūtra: The Wisdom of the Hour of Death
section: Verse Summary and Conclusion
author: Words of the Buddha
text: Since all things are pure of inherent nature, / Cultivate the perception of insubstantiality. / Since they are possessed of bodhicitta, / Cultivate the perception of great compassion. / Since all phenomena are by nature luminous, / Cultivate the perception of referencelessness. / Since all things are impermanent, / Cultivate the perception of non-attachment. / The mind is what causes wisdom's arising, / And so, look not for buddhahood elsewhere!
summary: The verse form summarizes the five perceptions, concluding that mind itself is the source of wisdom and buddhahood need not be sought externally.
notes: Verse form reinforces the prose teaching. Key vocabulary for word frequency: 'pure,' 'luminous,' 'impermanent,' 'wisdom,' 'buddhahood,' 'mind.' The directive 'look not for buddhahood elsewhere' positions mind as the locus of liberation. Translator: Lowell Cook, 2018.

title: The Noble Sūtra Teaching the Eleven Perceptions
section: Setting: The Buddha's Final Testament at Parinirvāṇa
author: Words of the Buddha
text: Thus did I hear at one time. The Blessed One was dwelling in the Grove of Twin Sāl-Trees in the vicinity of the Malla town of Kuśinagara. As the time came for his mahāparinirvāṇa, he addressed the monks: 'O monks, at the time of death, a monk should engender the eleven perceptions.'
summary: The sūtra is set at the moment of the Buddha's own parinirvāṇa at Kuśinagara, where he gives his final testament on perceptions to cultivate at the time of death.
notes: The sūtra's setting—at the moment of the Buddha's own death—gives particular authority to the teaching. Key vocabulary: 'mahāparinirvāṇa,' 'time of death,' 'perceptions.' Provides canonical Hīnayāna-adjacent reference for dying practices. Translator: Samye Translations (Lowell Cook/Stefan Mang), 2020.

title: The Noble Sūtra Teaching the Eleven Perceptions
section: Eleven Perceptions at the Time of Death
author: Words of the Buddha
text: What are these eleven? They are: 1. The perception of non-attachment to this life; 2. The perception of love for all beings; 3. The perception of relinquishing all grudges; 4. The perception of confessing all debauched discipline; 5. The perception of genuinely taking all the vows of discipline; 6. The perception of lightness towards even major wrongdoing; 7. The perception of greatness towards even minor roots of virtue; 8. The perception of fearlessness towards the next world; 9. The perception of impermanence towards all conditioned things; 10. The perception of selflessness toward all phenomena; 11. The perception of understanding nirvāṇa to be peace.
summary: The Buddha lists eleven perceptions to cultivate at death: non-attachment, love, releasing grudges, confession, discipline, ease with negativity, appreciation of virtue, fearlessness toward the next world, impermanence, selflessness, and nirvāṇa as peace.
notes: Provides a canonical checklist of death-moment mental attitudes. Key vocabulary: 'non-attachment,' 'impermanence,' 'selflessness,' 'nirvāṇa,' 'next world,' 'fearlessness.' Useful for comparing across sūtra and Vajrayana treatments of dying. Translator: Samye Translations, 2020.

title: Advice for Hor Özer
section: Distinction Between Ordinary Mind and Pure Awareness (Rigpa)
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: Generally speaking, the profound features of the Dzogchen instructions are without limit, but the fundamental point is the distinction between ordinary mind and pure awareness. Genuine awareness and the mind of vajra clear light are synonyms. In this tradition, however, recognition occurs directly in the midst of conceptualisation, on the principle that there is clear light even there, just as oil is present throughout a sesame seed. There is also a great difference in the means of sustaining that recognition. Nevertheless, without settling directly in pure awareness, merely to entertain the idea that all that arises is the self-expression of pure awareness will not bring about even so much as a semblance of genuine confidence in self-liberation.
summary: Dodrupchen III explains the crucial Dzogchen distinction between ordinary mind and pure awareness (rigpa), identifying rigpa with vajra clear light, and warns that mere conceptual acceptance of self-liberation is insufficient without direct recognition.
notes: Core Dzogchen epistemology: 'ordinary mind,' 'pure awareness,' 'rigpa,' 'clear light,' 'recognition,' 'self-liberation.' This section is foundational for understanding what recognition of awareness means in the bardo context. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2017.

title: Advice for Hor Özer
section: Self-Liberation of Thoughts in Clear Light
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: Even if you are not distracted from pure awareness, various virtuous and non-virtuous thoughts will still arise like waves in great number. Even though they arise in this way, you must remain unmoved from the natural resting place of the wisdom of pure awareness. Then, the force of that, will ensure that although conceptualisations suddenly arise in the first instant, they do not continue in the second. Instead, they will dissolve directly within the genuine sphere of clear light, and not bind the mental continuum. The reason that they do not bind the mind is not simply that thoughts do not continue in the second instant. Rather, it is based on the key point of applying the seal of the realisation of clear light as soon as a thought arises in the first instant.
summary: Thoughts dissolve within clear light when awareness is undistracted, rather than being suppressed; they arise in the first instant but self-liberate in the second instant through the seal of rigpa.
notes: Key for research: 'self-liberation,' 'clear light,' 'awareness,' 'thoughts,' 'dissolve.' The vocabulary of thought-arising and self-liberation in clear light directly parallels bardo recognition. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2017.

title: Advice for Hor Özer
section: Mindfulness in Dzogchen: Effortless vs. Conditioned
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: The common concentration of resting the mind has an aspect of stillness, but involves a temporary support and is therefore weak. By contrast, the concentration of self-abiding pure awareness is an innate property of the genuine nature, so it is not separate from the 'fluidity' of dharmatā. This means that there is no deliberate recollection of an object of focus—what we call 'conditioned mindfulness'—within pure awareness itself. Instead, there is effortless, naturally present mindfulness or intrinsic, uncontrived mindfulness. That is to say, clear light is maintained and there is what we call 'genuine mindfulness that prevents straying into the expressions of awareness'.
summary: Dodrupchen III distinguishes conditioned mindfulness from the innate, effortless mindfulness inherent in pure awareness, which is the 'fluid' quality of dharmatā and cannot be separated from clear light.
notes: Links 'mindfulness,' 'dharmatā,' 'clear light,' and 'awareness' in a technical discourse—useful for co-occurrence analysis. Positions dharmatā as the experiential ground even in this life. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2017.

title: Advice for Hor Özer
section: Stages of Dissolution at Death in Dzogchen
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: The various stages from the dissolution of the earth element into the earth element through to the dissolution of consciousness into space, space into clear light, clear light into union, union into wisdom, and wisdom into spontaneous presence, as well as ultimately how spontaneous presence is absorbed into the inner sphere, are taught clearly and in detail in Treasury of the Supreme Vehicle and in summary in Yeshe Lama. For followers of this Dharma tradition, the stages of dissolution at the time of death are as outlined above. At that time, prior orientation is important. Comprehensive transmission as if for a traveller about to cross a mountain pass means that one receives instructions on the bardos from one's teacher or a vajra sibling, and these instructions are made clear.
summary: Dodrupchen III summarizes the full dissolution sequence unique to Dzogchen: from elements through consciousness, space, clear light, union, wisdom, and spontaneous presence, emphasizing the importance of prior instruction for navigating it.
notes: Uniquely extends the dissolution sequence beyond the standard four-element model to include space, clear light, union, wisdom, and spontaneous presence. Key vocabulary: 'dissolution,' 'consciousness,' 'clear light,' 'spontaneous presence.' Critical comparative data. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2017.

title: Advice for Hor Özer
section: Practical Guidance: Before, During, and After Meditation
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: No matter what virtuous activity you might be engaged in, it is important to carry it out having first eliminated worldly distractions. During actual meditative equipoise, you must first lay bare pure awareness. Then settle naturally, without making any attempt to adjust, transform, reject or adopt anything. Should thoughts arise, do not indulge them, but maintain your ground within awareness—this is a crucial point. No matter what positive or negative experiences might arise, including bliss, clarity and absence of thought, as soon as you follow them with a judgement, you have strayed from the natural resting place of pure awareness and are already lost in its expressions.
summary: The text provides practical guidance for the three phases of Dzogchen practice: eliminating distraction beforehand, resting in bare awareness during, and maintaining the ground within awareness when thoughts arise.
notes: The practical vocabulary of resting in pure awareness—'natural resting place,' 'awareness,' 'judgement,' 'expressions'—is directly applicable to bardo recognition. Useful for word frequency of practice-instruction language. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2017.

title: Advice for a Dying Practitioner
section: Releasing Attachment and Confession
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: You will need to make preparations before the time comes to pass away. Think to yourself again and again: 'Whether death comes sooner or later, ultimately there is no alternative but to give up this body and all my possessions. This is just how it is for the world as a whole.' Thinking along these lines, sever completely the bonds of desire and attachment. Confess all the harmful actions you have committed in this and all your other lives, as well as any downfalls or breakages of vows you may have incurred, wittingly or unwittingly, and make repeated pledges never to act in such a way in future.
summary: Dodrupchen III advises that preparation for death begins with repeatedly contemplating the inevitability of giving up body and possessions, completely severing attachment, and confessing all harmful actions.
notes: Models the vocabulary of practical preparation for dying: 'death,' 'attachment,' 'body,' 'confession,' 'vows.' Critical for studying how dying preparation is framed in advice literature. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2006.

title: Advice for a Dying Practitioner
section: Cultivating Joy and Making Aspiration
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: Don't feel nervous or apprehensive about death. Try instead to raise your spirits and cultivate a clear sense of joy, bringing to mind all the positive, virtuous things you have done in the past. Without feeling any trace of pride or arrogance, celebrate your achievements over and again. Dedicate all your merits and make repeated prayers of aspiration, so that in all your future lives you may be able to take to heart the complete path of the supreme vehicle, with the guidance of a virtuous spiritual friend.
summary: Rather than fearing death, the practitioner is advised to cultivate joy by remembering past virtue, repeatedly dedicating merit, and praying to follow the complete path of the supreme vehicle in future lives.
notes: Joy as a psychological orientation at dying contrasts with fear; key vocabulary: 'joy,' 'death,' 'merit,' 'aspiration,' 'future lives.' Useful for studying the affective register of dying instruction literature. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2006.

title: Advice for a Dying Practitioner
section: Strengthening Aspiration and Devotion
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: In order to accomplish more easily whatever prayers you have made or intentions you have formed, it is profoundly beneficial to rely upon an embodiment of spiritual power. Bring to mind therefore the one for whom you have the greatest devotion, or to whom you feel the deepest connection through your practice, whether it is the great and glorious master of Oḍḍiyāna, Guru Rinpoche, or Noble Avalokiteśvara, the Lord of the World, and, with the confident trust that he or she is the embodiment of all the precious sources of refuge, pray one-pointedly for the fulfilment of your aspirations.
summary: To strengthen aspiration, Dodrupchen recommends one-pointed devotion to Guru Rinpoche or Avalokiteśvara as the embodiment of all refuge, praying with confidence that aspirations will be fulfilled.
notes: Establishes devotion as a death-moment support mechanism. Key vocabulary: 'devotion,' 'aspiration,' 'refuge,' 'Guru Rinpoche,' 'Avalokiteśvara.' Relevant for studying the role of refuge objects in the bardo transition. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2006.

title: Advice for a Dying Practitioner
section: Choosing Meditation at the Moment of Death
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Tenpe Nyima
text: At the actual moment of death it will be difficult to gather sufficient strength of mind to meditate on something new or unfamiliar, which is why you must choose an appropriate meditation beforehand and train until you are familiar with it. Then, as you pass away, you should devote your thoughts to the meditation as much as you possibly can, whether it is remembering the Buddha, focusing on the feeling of compassion, cultivating the view of śūnyatā, or remembering the Dharma or the Saṅgha. In order for this to happen successfully, it is also important that you train yourself beforehand to think, 'From now on, as I pass through this critical juncture of the time of death, I will not allow any negative thoughts to enter my mind.'
summary: Familiarity with one's chosen meditation before death is crucial; at the actual moment, one should maintain the most familiar practice—whether remembering the Buddha, compassion, or śūnyatā—and train the mind beforehand to exclude negative thoughts.
notes: Practical guidance on the role of prior meditation training for death. Key vocabulary: 'moment of death,' 'meditation,' 'consciousness,' 'compassion,' 'śūnyatā,' 'familiar.' Emphasizes the role of habituation in determining what arises at death. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2006.

title: Aspiration at the Moment of Death
section: Taking Refuge and Aspiring for All Beings
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: I take refuge in the body, speech, mind, qualities and activity of the noble gurus, who embody the Three Jewels and Three Roots. I supplicate you: inspire me with your blessings, I pray! Fulfil these aspirations of mine, I pray! From this life onwards, throughout all my future lives, may I never develop even the slightest negative intention towards sentient beings, who are my very own parents! May my positive intentions and actions flow uninterruptedly like a stream!
summary: Made at the approach of death, the text takes refuge in the gurus as embodiment of all refuge and aspires never to develop harmful intentions toward any being across all future lives.
notes: The aspiration's autobiographical context—composed at the approach of death—is particularly relevant to studying deathbed vocabulary. Key terms: 'refuge,' 'future lives,' 'positive intention,' 'aspirations.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2018.

title: Aspiration at the Moment of Death
section: Vows at the Moment of Death
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: With the exception of the pure gaṇacakra feast on the path of Vajrayāna, which does not involve any desire to eat, may I never consume or be involved in the consumption of even the slightest trace of flesh or blood from any living being! May I always recall these vows as vividly as I do now! For as long as I lack the two qualities of possession and freedom, may I never incur even the slightest karmic debt based on the misuse of offerings!
summary: The aspiration includes specific vows at the moment of death: never consuming flesh or blood except in feast-offering contexts, and never incurring karmic debt through misuse of offerings.
notes: Unusual vocabulary for death-moment context: 'vows,' 'karmic debt,' 'offerings,' 'flesh and blood.' Provides Vajrayana ethical framework at death that influences future rebirth. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2018.

title: Aspiration at the Moment of Death
section: Closing Aspiration Through the Power of Truth
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: May I be able to sacrifice my own body, speech and mind without the slightest impediment, even it is for the sake of just a single sentient being! Through the power of the truth of the unchanging dharmatā nature and the infallible law of causality, may these selfless aspirations be entirely fulfilled! Through the truth of the Three Jewels, may they be fulfilled! Through the truth of the noble gurus, may they be fulfilled!
summary: The aspiration closes by invoking the power of dharmatā's unchanging nature and the law of causality to fulfil these selfless aspirations, sealed by the truth of the Three Jewels and the gurus.
notes: Invokes 'dharmatā' as a ground of truth—unusual co-occurrence with aspiration prayer genre. Key vocabulary: 'dharmatā,' 'causality,' 'selfless,' 'truth,' 'Three Jewels.' Adds to corpus of dharmatā-as-ground usage. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2018.

title: The Excellent Path to Perfect Liberation
section: Invocation and Summoning the Consciousness of the Deceased
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: Hrīḥ. I appear clearly in the form of the Great Compassionate One. In my heart is the syllable hrīḥ, from which there emanate rays of light, Summoning the consciousness of the deceased, from the seven realms Or wherever it may reside, so that it dissolves into this form. Then, gently, in the presence of the deceased, recite the following: Hrīḥ. You who have died and passed beyond, now listen well! Wherever you are reborn among the six classes, it will only be a place of suffering. To escape, remain securely in this material support for the three doors—A form and name to which you are attached.
summary: The officiant appears as Avalokiteśvara and emits rays of light from the hrīḥ syllable to summon the consciousness of the deceased from wherever it is in the six realms, urging it to listen and remain in the ritual support.
notes: Rich ritual vocabulary: 'consciousness,' 'summoning,' 'six realms,' 'rebirth,' 'suffering,' 'the three doors.' Establishes the ritual logic of guiding the deceased consciousness. Note: Restricted Vajrayana text; requires empowerment. Translator: Rigpa Translations, 2014.

title: The Excellent Path to Perfect Liberation
section: Ablution and Purification of the Deceased
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: Hūṃ. Through the flowing water of the nirmāṇakāya's manifest display, / The expression of loving kindness and affection, / May all the impurity and obscurations of your / Aggregates, elements and sense-sources be cleansed and purified! / Hūṃ. Through the flowing water of the unceasing saṃbhogakāya, / The expression of the spontaneously perfect, all-pervading space, / May all the impurity and obscurations of your / Eight consciousnesses be cleansed and purified!
summary: The ritual bathes the consciousness of the deceased with the waters of the three kāyas, purifying the aggregates, elements, sense-sources, and eight consciousnesses, removing karmic obscurations accumulated since beginningless time.
notes: Unique vocabulary of karmic purification at death: 'aggregates,' 'elements,' 'eight consciousnesses,' 'obscurations,' 'purified.' The three-kāya water symbolism provides a structural framework linking dying to liberation. Restricted Vajrayana text. Translator: Rigpa Translations, 2014.

title: The Excellent Path to Perfect Liberation
section: Guidance Through the Six Realms
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: Hūṃ. Listen, deceased one, you who have left the body, child of noble family, / Through the fault of clinging to a self in the selfless nature of the mind, / And an 'other' in appearances which are devoid of any other, / Arises the terrifying inner and outer world—the environment and beings—of the hot and cold hells. / In reality, as this is but the delusion of your very own mind, / Know it to be deluded experience, / And, like waking from a dream, abandon these infernal realms! / Abandon the land of the pretas and seek instead the path to liberation and pure realms!
summary: In sequence, the deceased is addressed for each of the six realms, told that the suffering of each realm arises from a specific klesha and is ultimately the delusion of their own mind—like waking from a dream—and invited to seek liberation.
notes: Central to the research: the refrain 'delusion of your own mind,' 'like waking from a dream,' and 'seek liberation' appear for each of six realms. Key vocabulary: 'rebirth,' 'delusion,' 'consciousness,' 'liberation,' 'realms.' Restricted Vajrayana text. Translator: Rigpa Translations, 2014.

title: The Excellent Path to Perfect Liberation
section: Empowerment of the Three Kāyas
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: Oṃ. Outwardly the vase and its contents, / Inwardly the flowing nectar of the compassion / Of Padmapāṇi, the Holder of the Lotus— / By bestowing this empowerment on the body of the deceased, / May it ripen as the nirmāṇakāya! / Āḥ. Outwardly the sound of earth, water, fire and wind, / Inwardly the languages of the six classes of beings, / Secretly the secret speech of all the buddhas— / By bestowing this empowerment on the speech of the deceased, / May it ripen as the saṃbhogakāya! / Hūṃ. This material substance of the vajra / Symbolizes the enlightened mind of all the buddhas— / By bestowing this empowerment on the mind of the deceased, / May it ripen as the dharmakāya!
summary: Three empowerments are bestowed on the body, speech, and mind of the deceased, aspiring that each ripen as nirmāṇakāya, sambhogakāya, and dharmakāya respectively, leading to liberation.
notes: Key vocabulary: 'nirmāṇakāya,' 'saṃbhogakāya,' 'dharmakāya,' 'empowerment,' 'ripening,' 'liberation,' 'mind.' Shows the three-kāya structure as the organizing frame for post-death liberation. Restricted Vajrayana text. Translator: Rigpa Translations, 2014.

title: The Excellent Path to Perfect Liberation
section: Transference to Sukhāvatī
author: Dodrupchen Jigme Trinle Özer
text: Hūṃ! Deceased one, son or daughter of noble family, listen well! All the realms of saṃsāra, high or low, / Are like dungeons of the three sufferings, / In which all are imprisoned—this is certain. / So do not grasp! Do not be attached! But generate the supreme resolve. / To the west of here there lies / A place of perfect happiness and enjoyment, / A land of great bliss, / Where even the name of suffering is unknown, / Wherein dwells the Lord of Boundless Life. / Your mind itself is the syllable hrīḥ. / Swiftly now it moves, like a shooting star. / And in the expanse of Amitābha's wisdom mind, / Merges indivisibly, of a single taste.
summary: The consciousness of the deceased is urged to release all grasping and, as the syllable hrīḥ, to fly like a shooting star to the western pure land of Amitābha and merge indivisibly with his wisdom mind.
notes: Describes the phowa (transference) moment: consciousness as syllable, movement to Sukhāvatī, and merging with Amitābha. Key vocabulary: 'consciousness,' 'phowa,' 'liberation,' 'Amitābha,' 'pure land,' 'rebirth.' Restricted Vajrayana text. Translator: Rigpa Translations, 2014.

title: Transferring the Consciousness of the Deceased
section: Addressing the Deceased: Impermanence and Death
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: [Name of the deceased], having experienced impermanence you are now dead. You are not the only person to have experienced what we call 'death'; it is the path every one of the five kinds of sentient being must follow. Once birth has taken place, life can only end in death—such is the nature of things. There is not a single sentient being in this world—from the rich and powerful universal monarch to the starving beggar unable even to feed himself—who can free themselves from life and death.
summary: Jigme Lingpa opens by directly addressing the deceased by name, naming them dead and universalizing the experience of death across all five types of sentient beings.
notes: Direct address genre vocabulary: 'dead,' 'death,' 'impermanence,' 'sentient beings,' 'life and death,' 'nature of things.' Models the linguistic register of speaking directly to the dead. Translator: Gyurme Avertin, Adam Pearcey, Han Kop, 2023.

title: Transferring the Consciousness of the Deceased
section: Karma and the Power of Faith at Death
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: Now that you are personally experiencing the ripening of the truth of suffering described in this verse, which is the very nature of things, the danger is that your attachment to your relatives, wealth and possessions will lead you to a lower realm. We should rejoice at being able to experience death and transference during this fortunate time when the teachings of the Buddha exist in this world. Because simply to hear the names of the bhagavān buddhas is very powerful. This means that those who pass away while hearing these names and experiencing trust and joy will be liberated from lower rebirths and attain the level of a god or human being.
summary: Warning against attachment leading to lower rebirth, Jigme Lingpa encourages the deceased to rejoice that they can experience death with the Buddha's teachings present, noting that even hearing buddha-names at death brings liberation from lower rebirths.
notes: Key vocabulary: 'attachment,' 'lower rebirth,' 'karma,' 'liberation,' 'death,' 'consciousness,' 'trust and joy.' Establishes the soteriological stakes at the death moment. Translator: Gyurme Avertin, Adam Pearcey, Han Kop, 2023.

title: Transferring the Consciousness of the Deceased
section: Relying on Amitābha and Aspiring to Sukhāvatī
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: The protector Amitābha, in particular, promised that all fear in the bardos and all the causes of lower rebirth will be completely eliminated for anyone who remembers or hears his name, and abides by the view. So rely on him and the bodhisattvas Avalokiteśvara and Mahāsthāmaprāpta, and do not forget their buddhafield, Sukhāvatī. Since you are now free of your physical body of flesh and blood, go straight to this supreme buddhafield. Think of and meditate on the guru for whom you have devotion. Remember your yidam and the view.
summary: Amitābha's vow to eliminate fear in the bardos and all causes of lower rebirth is invoked; the deceased is instructed to rely on him and the two bodhisattvas and to go directly to Sukhāvatī.
notes: Directly links Amitābha's vow to bardo-fear and lower rebirth—key for research. Vocabulary: 'bardos,' 'fear,' 'lower rebirth,' 'Sukhāvatī,' 'buddhafield,' 'consciousness,' 'devotion.' Translator: Gyurme Avertin, Adam Pearcey, Han Kop, 2023.

title: Transferring the Consciousness of the Deceased
section: Instruction on Transference: The Syllable A
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: One method for creating auspicious circumstances through these key points is the instruction on transference, which brings enlightenment without meditation and which is applied at the juncture between one lifetime and the next. Meditate, therefore, on a white syllable 'a' in your heart. As I repeat the sound 'a', bring into your experience all that I have just said. Consider that the 'a' goes straight to the buddhafield of Sukhāvatī, like an arrow shot by a powerful archer. Apply the syllable 'a', / Combined with the breath, as many times as the person's age. / If they do not die at that very moment, / Then with breath directed towards the crown, / Gather their consciousness into the middle of the body / While focusing on the syllable 'a'.
summary: The instruction on phowa (consciousness transference) uses the syllable 'a' at the heart; repeated as many times as the deceased's age while applying it to breath, it propels consciousness to Sukhāvatī like an arrow.
notes: Central description of phowa practice: 'consciousness,' 'transference,' 'syllable a,' 'breath,' 'Sukhāvatī,' 'liberation.' The 'like an arrow' metaphor appears in multiple bardo texts. Translator: Gyurme Avertin, Adam Pearcey, Han Kop, 2023.

title: The Nail of Taking Death as a Friend
section: Introduction: Taking Death as a Friend vs. Enemy
author: Marpa Chökyi Lodrö
text: When these methods do not work in preventing death and a yogi meets their end, death should be taken as a friend. Worldly beings grasp death as their enemy, whereas yogis, on the basis of the guru's oral instructions, will at the time of death, or in the bardo, take it as a friend, enabling them to attain the siddhi of Mahāmudrā.
summary: Marpa introduces the teaching by contrasting the worldly perception of death as enemy with the yogi's orientation of taking death as a friend through guru instructions, leading to Mahāmudrā accomplishment.
notes: Frames the text's central argument: death as opportunity for liberation. Key vocabulary: 'death,' 'bardo,' 'friend,' 'Mahāmudrā,' 'siddhi,' 'guru.' Provides the Kagyu tradition's orientation toward dying. Translator: Sean Ross, 2023.

title: The Nail of Taking Death as a Friend
section: The Natural Process of Dying
author: Marpa Chökyi Lodrö
text: For the first, both when death happens gradually and when it happens all of a sudden, the following signs emerge. At the time the earth element dissolves into the water element the outer sign is that there is a heaviness of the body. The inner sign is that smoke emerges. When fire dissolves into wind, the outer sign is that the body's heat dissipates, beginning at its extremities. The inner sign is that the appearance of fireflies arises. When wind dissolves into consciousness the outer sign is that breathing is weak, the inner sign is that outer appearances fade, and consciousness abides clearly like a butter lamp within a vase. When consciousness dissolves into luminosity the outer sign is that the person is pronounced dead. The inner sign is that death, the Prajñāpāramitā, luminous dharmakāya as it is, dawns right there at once.
summary: Marpa describes the natural dying process as the dissolution of earth, fire, wind, and consciousness with matching outer (bodily) and inner (visionary) signs, culminating in consciousness abiding like a butter lamp and then luminous dharmakāya dawning.
notes: Notable formulation: 'death, the Prajñāpāramitā, luminous dharmakāya.' Key vocabulary: 'element dissolves,' 'consciousness,' 'luminosity,' 'dharmakāya,' 'signs.' The butter-lamp-in-a-vase image parallels the youthful vase body in T06. Translator: Sean Ross, 2023.

title: The Nail of Taking Death as a Friend
section: Taking Death onto the Path: Preliminaries and Main Practice
author: Marpa Chökyi Lodrö
text: For the common branch, depend upon a guru or Dharma friend to refresh the power of the oral instructions. Gather the accumulations by offering your possessions. Visualize yourself as your yidam deity, resting your mind single-pointedly in whatever you have experienced of the completion stage practices. For the main practice, through habituating yourself to luminosity, like the merging of a river and the ocean, or the meeting of a baby calf and its mother, your uncontrived ordinary mind, mother and son, unite as one.
summary: The preliminary practices involve depending on the guru, gathering accumulations, and yidam visualization; the main practice is habituating to luminosity until ordinary mind (mother) and path luminosity (son) merge like a river entering the ocean.
notes: The 'mother and child luminosity' metaphor appears here in Kagyu formulation. Key vocabulary: 'luminosity,' 'mother and child,' 'ordinary mind,' 'completion stage,' 'yidam.' Important for cross-tradition comparison. Translator: Sean Ross, 2023.

title: The Nail of Taking Death as a Friend
section: Conclusion: Liberation or Return Through the Bardo
author: Marpa Chökyi Lodrö
text: After your own experience of luminosity and the characteristic of the luminosity of the bardo unify as one, finally at death, the Prajñāpāramitā, luminous dharmakāya, will be attained. If that doesn't happen, the illusory body of the bardo remains for seven days or seven weeks. From this, during each of these periods of seven, an instance of natural luminosity will occur. If luminosity and the illusory body are not recognized, you will circle in the three realms. Through the force of the yogi's previous momentum, the recognition of luminosity and the illusory body dawn at this time without fail. From the power of the emergence of that recognition, the kāya of unified great Vajradhara will be attained.
summary: If luminosity is recognized at death, dharmakāya is attained; if not, the illusory bardo body remains for seven-day periods with repeated luminosity opportunities; recognition eventually leads to the kāya of unified Vajradhara.
notes: Describes the full arc from dying to liberation or return through the bardo. Key vocabulary: 'luminosity,' 'bardo,' 'recognition,' 'illusory body,' 'dharmakāya,' 'Vajradhara,' 'three realms.' Translator: Sean Ross, 2023.

title: A Wondrous Instruction for the Moment of Death
section: Preliminaries: Refuge, Bodhicitta, and Amitābha Visualization
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: First take refuge in the Three Jewels, / Then cultivate bodhicitta, awakening-mind, / Visualize Amitābha before you, / And pay homage and make offerings to him. / These are the preliminaries.
summary: The instruction's preliminaries are: taking refuge, generating bodhicitta, visualizing Amitābha before oneself, and making homage and offerings.
notes: Concise statement of Sakya Amitābha practice preliminaries. Key vocabulary: 'refuge,' 'bodhicitta,' 'Amitābha,' 'visualization.' Representative of the Sakya tradition's approach to death-moment practice. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: A Wondrous Instruction for the Moment of Death
section: Main Part: Meditation Practice on Amitābha at Death
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: Generate conviction that your misdeeds have been purified. / Let go of all forms of attachment. / And with a mind lacking any fear of death, / Practice the meditation on Amitābha. / This is the main part.
summary: The main practice at the moment of death is to cultivate confidence in the purification of misdeeds, release all attachment, and maintain fearlessness while practicing Amitābha meditation.
notes: Three elements of the death-moment practice: purification confidence, non-attachment, fearlessness. Key vocabulary: 'purification,' 'attachment,' 'fear of death,' 'Amitābha,' 'meditation.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: A Wondrous Instruction for the Moment of Death
section: Conclusion: Dissolution into Amitābha
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: Amitābha melts and dissolves into you, / So that you take on his essential nature. / Dedicate the virtue to sentient beings. / Then train in this all over again. / This is the conclusion.
summary: In the conclusion, Amitābha melts into the practitioner so that the practitioner takes on his essential nature, and virtue is dedicated to all sentient beings.
notes: Dissolves into you'—uses dissolution language for deity absorption, paralleling elemental dissolution at death. Key vocabulary: 'dissolves,' 'essential nature,' 'virtue,' 'dedication.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: A Wondrous Instruction for the Moment of Death
section: Benefits: No Suffering at Death and Bardo Protection
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: Illness and harmful influences will not arise, / And at the time of death there'll be no suffering. / In the bardos you'll be cared for by the buddhas. / And thereafter you will attain liberation. / These are the benefits.
summary: The benefits of the Amitābha meditation practice are freedom from illness, no suffering at death, protection by the buddhas in the bardos, and eventual liberation.
notes: Co-occurrence: 'death,' 'suffering,' 'bardos,' 'buddhas,' 'liberation.' Direct claim that practice leads from death through bardo to liberation—central to the research question. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: Meditation on Amitābha
section: Opening Verse from the Prayer of Good Actions
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: From the Good Actions: / When the time comes for me to die, / Let all that obscures me fade away, so / I look on Amitābha, there in person, / And go at once to his pure land of Sukhāvatī. / In that pure land, may I actualize every single one / Of all these aspirations!
summary: The opening quotation from the Prayer of Good Actions aspires that at the moment of death, all obscurations fade so that one beholds Amitābha in person and goes directly to Sukhāvatī.
notes: Canonical aspiration for pure-land rebirth at death. Key vocabulary: 'die,' 'obscurations,' 'Amitābha,' 'pure land,' 'Sukhāvatī,' 'aspirations.' Provides the scriptural anchor for the following practice. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: Meditation on Amitābha
section: Visualization of Amitābha and the Pure Land
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: When going to sleep at night, lie down on your right side and practise taking refuge and generating bodhicitta. Imagine that the place you are in is the pure-land of Sukhāvatī, and visualise yourself as your yidam deity. Consider that before you, on a lotus and moon-disc is Amitābha, red in colour, with his two hands in the mudrā of equanimity and holding an alms-bowl filled with amṛta-nectar. His legs are crossed and he is adorned with various jewelled ornaments. Imagine that he is surrounded by gurus, and, beyond them, buddhas and bodhisattvas.
summary: The practice begins each night with visualizing one's surroundings as Sukhāvatī, oneself as the yidam deity, and Amitābha on a lotus before one—red, in equanimity, with nectar-bowl, surrounded by buddhas.
notes: Detailed visualization language: 'Sukhāvatī,' 'yidam,' 'Amitābha,' 'lotus,' 'equanimity,' 'nectar.' Sleeping practice as parallel to the dying process (consciousness leaving and entering). Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: Meditation on Amitābha
section: Breathing Meditation: Mind Merging with Amitābha
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: Then, as you breathe out, consider that your own mind dissolves into the Buddha's heart, merging with it inseparably. As you breathe in, consider that light emanates from the Buddha's heart, and, following the pathway of your speech, dissolves into your heart, so that the buddha's wisdom-mind merges inseparably with your own mind. Repeat this process three times.
summary: On each out-breath the practitioner's mind dissolves into Amitābha's heart; on each in-breath light from his heart dissolves into the practitioner's heart—three times, merging both minds inseparably.
notes: Uses 'dissolves' and 'merges' vocabulary parallel to death and bardo. Key co-occurrence: 'mind,' 'dissolves,' 'heart,' 'wisdom-mind,' 'merges,' 'light.' Breathing practice as rehearsal for consciousness transference. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: Meditation on Amitābha
section: Concluding Dissolution and Aspiration
author: Sakya Paṇḍita Kunga Gyaltsen
text: Then, at the end, consider that the buddhas and bodhisattvas dissolve into the gurus. The gurus then dissolve into Amitābha. Amitābha, in turn, melts into light and dissolves into you. You too melt into light, imagining that the Buddha, yidam deity and your own mind all merge together inseparably. Recite the following prayer of aspiration: / Born there in a beautiful lotus flower, / In that excellent and joyous buddha realm, / May the Buddha Amitābha himself / Grant me the prophecy foretelling my enlightenment!
summary: The practice concludes with a sequential dissolution: bodhisattvas into gurus, gurus into Amitābha, Amitābha into the practitioner, and the practitioner into light—all merging inseparably, followed by an aspiration for lotus birth in Sukhāvatī.
notes: The full dissolution sequence mirrors the dying process: dissolution from outside in. Key vocabulary: 'dissolves,' 'light,' 'lotus birth,' 'Sukhāvatī,' 'prophecy,' 'enlightenment.' Models the path from death to liberation through Amitābha. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2016.

title: Fostering Recollection to Dispel the Torment of Deluded Perception
section: Supplication to the Guru and General Aspiration
author: Jamyang Khyentse Chökyi Lodrö
text: Care for me at all times with your magnificent love, alleviate all the terrors of deluded perception during this life, the next and the bardo states, inspire us to recognise the bardos and lead us to a pure realm, we pray. / You protect from all the torridity and torment of conditioned existence, / And the mere thought of you closes the door to lower realms, / Revealer of the path to liberation, gracious lord of Dharma, / Supreme guide, great face and embodiment of all sources of refuge, care for us!
summary: The prayer opens with a supplication to the guru to protect against the terrors of deluded perception in this life, the next, and the bardos, and to lead the practitioner to a pure realm through recognizing the bardos.
notes: The phrase 'recognise the bardos' captures the core aspiration of the text. Key vocabulary: 'bardos,' 'recognition,' 'deluded perception,' 'liberation,' 'lower realms,' 'pure realm.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2020.

title: Fostering Recollection to Dispel the Torment of Deluded Perception
section: The Approach of Death: Mindfulness and Instruction
author: Jamyang Khyentse Chökyi Lodrö
text: When it comes time to die, may we not experience a sudden, painful end, / But instead hold the natural state of awareness / And pray to the guru with yearning faith and devotion, / While we practically apply the instructions for the moment of death. / Holding the hook of mindfulness and vigilance during meditation and thereafter, / May we recognise all these deluded perceptions / As the appearances of the intermediate state / And thus apply the path to the bardo of birth and death.
summary: At the approach of death, the prayer aspires to maintain natural awareness and the guru's instructions, using mindfulness to recognize all arising perceptions as bardo appearances and apply them to the path.
notes: Captures the role of mindfulness and instruction-recollection at the death moment. Key vocabulary: 'awareness,' 'mindfulness,' 'recognition,' 'bardo,' 'deluded perceptions,' 'appearances.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2020.

title: Fostering Recollection to Dispel the Torment of Deluded Perception
section: Bardo of Dying: Elemental Dissolution and Three Experiences
author: Jamyang Khyentse Chökyi Lodrö
