text: When the appearances of this life are about to fade away, / The five winds will dissolve, the five chakras disintegrate. / Physical strength will diminish, the sound of voices grow unclear, / Memory will deteriorate and consciousness become dim. / Sensory awareness will fade and objects become imperceptible. / The subtle stages of dissolution are appearance, increase and attainment. / When consciousness dissolves into appearance, / The white element descends and there is total whiteness. / The thirty-three thought-states associated with anger cease. / At that time may we recognise the wisdom of appearance.
summary: As the appearances of this life fade, the five winds dissolve, chakras disintegrate, and consciousness dims; the prayer aspires to recognize each of the three subtle experiences—appearance, increase, attainment—as their respective wisdoms.
notes: Combines coarse and subtle dissolution vocabulary in aspiration prayer form. Key vocabulary: 'dissolves,' 'consciousness,' 'wisdom,' 'appearance,' 'increase,' 'attainment,' 'white element,' 'recognition.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2020.

title: Fostering Recollection to Dispel the Torment of Deluded Perception
section: Clear Light: Meeting of Mother and Child Luminosity
author: Jamyang Khyentse Chökyi Lodrö
text: Then, when the ground luminosity of near-attainment dawns, / Beyond ordinary mental objects and clear as an autumn sky, / May the child-like path luminosity / Meet the mother that is the clear light of the ground. / If, due to our weak familiarity at that stage, / We fail in recognition, then, in an instant, / The appearances of the bardo of becoming will dawn.
summary: As near-attainment dissolves into ground luminosity, the prayer aspires for the child luminosity of the path to meet the mother clear light of the ground; if recognition fails, the bardo of becoming dawns instantly.
notes: Central aspiration for the meeting of mother and child luminosity—the key recognition event. Key vocabulary: 'ground luminosity,' 'mother and child,' 'clear light,' 'recognition,' 'bardo of becoming.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2020.

title: Fostering Recollection to Dispel the Torment of Deluded Perception
section: Bardo of Becoming and Aspiration for Fortunate Rebirth
author: Jamyang Khyentse Chökyi Lodrö
text: When the four dreadful sounds, three terrifying abysses, / And six uncertain experiences arise, / May we recollect the guru, the deity and the view, / And take rebirth in a pure celestial realm. / Even if we are connected to a place of birth, / May we close the womb-door of any lesser form of rebirth / And seek the excellent support of a dharma practitioner. / May we gain a form complete with the six elements / And meet a spiritual guide as soon as we are reborn.
summary: In the becoming bardo, amid the four dreadful sounds, three terrifying abysses, and six uncertainties, the prayer aspires to recall the guru and deity, attain a pure realm, close the womb door to lower rebirth, and take a dharma-practitioner's form.
notes: Comprehensive becoming-bardo vocabulary: 'dreadful sounds,' 'abysses,' 'womb door,' 'rebirth,' 'pure realm,' 'consciousness.' Also includes the aspiration for a dharma body—connecting rebirth to liberation over future lives. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2020.

title: The Single Oceanic Wisdom: An Aspiration to Recognise the Bardos
section: Opening Prayer and Bardo of This Life
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: Precious guru, embodiment of all the buddhas / Throughout the three times, / Turn your compassionate gaze towards me, / For I am without refuge and beset by deep-seated confusion. / Throughout time without beginning, I have been oppressed / By karma, destructive emotions and negative deeds. / Having won a life of leisure and fortune, / May I please the guru, and, / With wisdom arisen through study, contemplation and meditation, / Cultivate renunciation and bodhicitta, / And practice the supreme path of the six perfect actions.
summary: After a confession of karmic misdeeds, the prayer aspires to please the guru, cultivate wisdom through study-contemplation-meditation, and practice the six perfections in the bardo of this life.
notes: Establishes the foundation in renunciation and bodhicitta for all subsequent bardo recognitions. Key vocabulary: 'confusion,' 'karma,' 'wisdom,' 'renunciation,' 'bardo of this life.' Translator: Sean Price, 2021.

title: The Single Oceanic Wisdom: An Aspiration to Recognise the Bardos
section: Bardo of Dying: Transference and Elemental Dissolution
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: In time, I'll have to pass on / To the so-called bardo of dying. / Then may I not suffer / A painful, sudden or violent death, / But by unifying basic space and awareness, / Enter the sphere of luminosity / And be freed within the primordial ground. / However, should my familiarity prove weak, / May I close the eight gateways, open the aperture of brahma, / And, through familiarity with the instruction / On the ejection of consciousness riding the subtle wind, / Take birth in Khecara, celestial abode of the vidyādharas.
summary: The prayer aspires for a peaceful death by unifying space and awareness to enter luminosity; if that fails, phowa is applied—closing the eight gateways and ejecting consciousness to Khecara through the aperture of brahma.
notes: Describes two outcomes at the bardo of dying: recognition and liberation, or phowa to a pure realm. Key vocabulary: 'bardo of dying,' 'awareness,' 'luminosity,' 'phowa,' 'consciousness,' 'primordial ground.' Translator: Sean Price, 2021.

title: The Single Oceanic Wisdom: An Aspiration to Recognise the Bardos
section: Elemental Dissolution and the Three Experiences
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: Yet if I do not actualise the bodhisattvas' / Transference of consciousness in this way, / The earth element will dissolve into itself, and I'll be unable to rise; / The water element will dissolve into itself, and mucus will leak from my nose; / The fire element will dissolve into itself, and my mouth and nose will dry up; / And when the wind element dissolves, my breath will falter, and my limbs weaken. / Next, as a result of the separation / Of the gross channels, drops and subtle winds, / The subtle experiences of appearance, increase and attainment will arise. / With the descent of the white element, / Thoughts related to desire will cease.
summary: If phowa fails, the four elements dissolve with specific bodily signs, followed by the subtle dissolution through the three experiences of appearance (white), increase (red), and attainment, with corresponding cessation of thought-states.
notes: Full elemental and subtle dissolution vocabulary: 'dissolves,' 'earth,' 'water,' 'fire,' 'wind,' 'appearance,' 'increase,' 'attainment,' 'white element,' 'thoughts cease.' Parallels T04 and T17 for co-occurrence analysis. Translator: Sean Price, 2021.

title: The Single Oceanic Wisdom: An Aspiration to Recognise the Bardos
section: Bardo of Dharmatā: Recognition and Liberation
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: If I cannot merge with the dharmakāya / And seize the primordial expanse within, / The extremely subtle energy-mind / Will develop in reverse from near-attainment / To increase and appearance, and / As the primordially pure expanse of the basal dhātu, / The bardo of dharmatā, dawns, / May I realise it through the view of Trekchö. / As the natural luminosity of the ground, / All appearances will manifest as five-coloured light, like unfurled brocade. / Then, through my familiarity with Tögal, / May I recognise them as my own manifestation—without self-nature— / So that they are instantly exhausted within suchness.
summary: If liberation at the dharmakāya level fails, the subtle energy-mind reverses through the experiences as the dharmatā bardo dawns; through Trekchö view and Tögal practice, five-colored light appearances are recognized as one's own and instantly exhausted.
notes: Unique Dzogchen-specific vocabulary: 'Trekchö,' 'Tögal,' 'five-coloured light,' 'suchness,' 'exhausted.' Describes liberation through practice-specific recognition. Key for Dzogchen lexicon analysis. Translator: Sean Price, 2021.

title: The Single Oceanic Wisdom: An Aspiration to Recognise the Bardos
section: Bardo of Becoming and Final Aspiration
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: Yet, if I should wander into the bardo of becoming, / I will become an aggregate of the four 'names'— / With all senses intact and a body of mental energy. / I'll clearly recall my death and know that I've passed on, / Blown about like a feather borne upon the wind, / Barred only from the place of liberation and site of rebirth. / I'll follow the winds of karma, suffer exhaustion, / And, in terror, endure the torments of my death / Repeatedly, at the end of each and every week. / May I select an excellent entrance to future rebirth, / A physical form complete with the freedoms and advantages, / And by combining this with pure prayers of aspiration, / Obtain a form possessing the seven qualities of the higher realms.
summary: In the becoming bardo, the mental body of four 'names' wanders blown by karmic winds, dying every seven days; the prayer aspires to select a fortunate rebirth complete with freedoms and advantages through pure aspiration.
notes: Detailed becoming bardo vocabulary: 'mental body,' 'karmic winds,' 'seven days,' 'rebirth,' 'freedoms and advantages.' The 'feather in the wind' image evokes lack of agency. Key for bardo-of-becoming analysis. Translator: Sean Price, 2021.

title: Training in the Pure Realms of the Three Kāyas
section: Supplication to Guru Rinpoche and Meditation on Impermanence
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: Kyema Kyihü! O Lotus-born, master of pure awareness, / Sentient beings like me in this degenerate age possess evil karma. / When I yearn for happiness, yet contrive to create only suffering, / When my every effort is completely wrong, who can I turn to? / Life is precarious, like a chick perched on the edge of a cliff. / There's no certainty that death will not come tonight. / Planning to live forever, I am caught by the demon of distraction, / And when the henchmen of the Lord of Death come by, who will I turn to then? / Look on me with compassion, you who dwell in Ngayab Ling—Care for me, guide me, inspire me, make me one with you. / Lead me, right now, to the Copper-coloured Mountain of Glory!
summary: Jigme Lingpa opens with urgent supplication to Guru Rinpoche: life is as precarious as a chick at a cliff's edge, death could come tonight, and only Guru Rinpoche at the Copper-coloured Mountain can offer refuge.
notes: Strong vocabulary of impermanence, death-urgency, and guru-as-refuge. Key terms: 'death,' 'impermanence,' 'consciousness,' 'Copper-coloured Mountain,' 'Lord of Death.' Establishes the devotional context for the subsequent bardo aspiration. Rigpa Translations.

title: Training in the Pure Realms of the Three Kāyas
section: Moment of Death and Elemental Signs
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: Kyema! When my life force is spent, / The vital glow slips from my body, and breath comes in gasps, one upon the other, / When the inner supporting air withdraws, and my weak and groaning corpse severs / The links between me and loved ones in their grief, / Let me not suffer the fiercest agony, at the final moment of death, / But instead behold the ḍākinīs come to bid me welcome. / Kyihü! Earth, water, fire, air and space: as the five elements / Dissolve one by one, the visions of smoke, mirages, / Sparks and lamps become clear.
summary: At the final moment of death—when the vital glow leaves, breath falters, and connections to loved ones are severed—the prayer aspires to see the ḍākinīs rather than suffer agony, then describes the elemental dissolution signs.
notes: Combines the intimate, affective moment of dying (vital glow, loved ones, grief) with the standard dissolution signs (smoke, mirages, sparks, lamp). Key vocabulary: 'dying,' 'life force,' 'dissolution,' 'elements,' 'ḍākinīs.' Rigpa Translations.

title: Training in the Pure Realms of the Three Kāyas
section: Subtle Dissolution: Appearance, Increase, Attainment
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: And so, as consciousness dissolves into appearance, / Like an eclipse in a cloudless sky, / The red experience dawns, and red essence rises to the heart. / In turn, as appearance dissolves into increase, / Like moonbeams slanting through a skylight, / The white experience unfolds, and white essence descends. / Then, as increase dissolves into near attainment, / Like the darkness at dusk on a clear and cloudless night, / The black experience draws in, and I sink into the ālaya, the ground of all.
summary: The three subtle experiences unfold with precise imagery: red (eclipse) → white (moonbeams through skylight) → black (dusk darkness), with corresponding movement of red and white essences and the sinking of consciousness into the ālaya.
notes: Vivid imagistic language for the three subtle experiences—useful for tracking poetic registers. Key vocabulary: 'consciousness dissolves,' 'appearance,' 'increase,' 'attainment,' 'red,' 'white,' 'ālaya.' Note the reversal of red/white order vs. most texts. Rigpa Translations.

title: Training in the Pure Realms of the Three Kāyas
section: Ground Luminosity and Bardo of Dharmatā
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: Once again, with the eightfold separation of the life-supporting wind, / I awaken slightly from unconsciousness, and the original primordial radiance dawns, / Clear and unobstructed, like a limpid autumn sky. / While I rest in this state of empty cognizance, free from all obscuring veils, / In this moment, may I realize the primordial purity of nowness, the space that is free from conceptual mind, / As 'ordinary' awareness, fresh, vast and boundless. / And through the power of meditating in that state, / In that very instant may I seize the stronghold of / The space of the primordial ground.
summary: After the ālaya phase, the eightfold wind separation brings a momentary awakening into primordial radiance—clear, unobstructed, like an autumn sky—and the prayer aspires to seize the stronghold of the primordial ground at that very instant.
notes: The phrase 'ordinary awareness, fresh, vast and boundless' is a notable Dzogchen formulation for ground luminosity. Key vocabulary: 'primordial radiance,' 'awareness,' 'empty cognizance,' 'ground,' 'liberation.' Rigpa Translations.

title: Training in the Pure Realms of the Three Kāyas
section: Bardo of Dharmatā Visions and Liberation into Pure Realm
author: Jigme Lingpa
text: If I am not liberated in this, the first bardo, / The appearances of space dissolve into spontaneously present luminosity, / And when sound, light, rays, mandala patterns and the like arise— / The visions of the eightfold process of unfolding— / May I recognize them as my own appearances in the bardo of dharmatā, / And be liberated, like a child leaping into its mother's lap. / Yet if I am shocked by the sounds, terrified by the rays, / Frightened by the appearances of deities, and liberation eludes me, / Through the truth of the nature of reality, and through the blessing of the master, / May I be inspired and liberated, miraculously born / In the heart of a lotus flower in a natural nirmāṇakāya realm!
summary: If the first-bardo luminosity is missed, the eightfold dharmatā visions arise; recognition as own appearances brings liberation like a child into its mother's lap; otherwise, blessing-inspired lotus birth in a nirmāṇakāya realm.
notes: The 'child leaping into mother's lap' metaphor for recognition and liberation appears here. Key vocabulary: 'dharmatā,' 'visions,' 'recognition,' 'liberation,' 'lotus birth,' 'nirmāṇakāya.' Rigpa Translations.

title: Prayer for this Life the Next and the Bardos
section: Invocation of the Lineage and Aspiration for All Three States
author: Khenchen Ngawang Palzang
text: Vimalamitra, who realized the maṇḍala of the supreme state, / Longchenpa, who revealed the dharmakāya Samantabhadra, / And Pema Ledrel Tsal, to you I pray: / Hold me with compassion in this life, the next, and the bardo state!
summary: The prayer opens by invoking the Dzogchen lineage—Vimalamitra, Longchenpa, and Jigme Lingpa (Pema Ledrel Tsal)—and aspires for their compassionate protection across this life, the next life, and the bardo states.
notes: Invokes the Dzogchen lineage for bardo protection. Key vocabulary: 'dharmakāya,' 'Samantabhadra,' 'bardo state,' 'this life,' 'next life.' Short but defines the three-context framework. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: Prayer for this Life the Next and the Bardos
section: Bardo of Dying: Clear Light Recognition and Dharmakāya
author: Khenchen Ngawang Palzang
text: When the luminosity of the first bardo state arises, / May I recognise it, perfect the strength and gain stability, / Then, awakening in basic space with its six special features, / May I gain stability in the youthful vase body!
summary: When the luminosity of the first bardo (the dying bardo) arises, the prayer aspires to recognize it, perfect one's strength, awaken in the primordially pure basic space, and gain stability in the youthful vase body.
notes: Connects the luminosity of dying directly to the youthful vase body concept from T06. Key vocabulary: 'luminosity,' 'first bardo,' 'recognition,' 'basic space,' 'youthful vase body,' 'dharmakāya.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: Prayer for this Life the Next and the Bardos
section: Bardo of Dharmatā: Saṃbhogakāya Appearances
author: Khenchen Ngawang Palzang
text: When the saṃbhogakāya appearances that are rigpa's expression arise, / May I find certainty, just like a child entering its mother's lap, / And, realizing the key point of engaging with lights and awareness, / May I attain buddhahood in saṃbhogakāya form!
summary: When the sambhogakāya appearances—the expression of rigpa—arise in the dharmatā bardo, the prayer aspires to find certainty through the recognition of lights and awareness, attaining buddhahood in sambhogakāya form.
notes: Concise formulation of dharmatā bardo recognition and liberation. Key vocabulary: 'sambhogakāya,' 'rigpa,' 'lights,' 'awareness,' 'buddhahood.' The mother-child metaphor recurs. Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.

title: Prayer for this Life the Next and the Bardos
section: Bardo of Becoming: Liberation Through Pure Realm Aspiration
author: Khenchen Ngawang Palzang
text: When the karmic bardo of becoming arises, / Then, through relying upon the five means of enlightenment without meditation, / In a pure realm where the great relief of liberation is assured, / May I attain buddhahood in a single lifetime!
summary: When the karmic bardo of becoming arises, the prayer aspires to rely on the five means of enlightenment without meditation and attain buddhahood in a single lifetime in a pure realm.
notes: Succinct aspiration for the becoming bardo. 'Enlightenment without meditation' (liberation through hearing, wearing, tasting, touching, remembering) is a unique vocabulary cluster. Key vocabulary: 'bardo of becoming,' 'karma,' 'liberation,' 'pure realm,' 'buddhahood.' Translator: Adam Pearcey, 2019.
